destination.name: Wachau
destination.hero_image_alt: Wachau valley terraced vineyards with historic village nestled among the hills
destination.blurb: A UNESCO World Heritage valley carved by the Danube between Melk and Krems, where gneiss and loess terraces produce dry Grüner Veltliner and Riesling under the Steinfeder-Federspiel-Smaragd tier system.
destination.tagline: Danube terraces, gneiss soils, the Smaragd standard
destination.overview:
The Wachau stretches 36 kilometres along the Danube between Melk and Krems in Lower Austria, a landscape of terraced vineyards, apricot orchards, Baroque monasteries and castle ruins inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000. The region received its own Wachau DAC designation in 2020, formalising the geographic-origin appellation for wines made here. Before and alongside the DAC, the Vinea Wachau growers association has governed quality since 1983 through three tier marks: Steinfeder (the lightest style, named after the wild feather grass), Federspiel (mid-weight, named after the falconer's lure), and Smaragd (the ripest tier, named after the green emerald lizard found on the warm stone walls).

Grüner Veltliner and Riesling share the region's roughly 1,400 hectares of vines, planted on primary rock terraces of gneiss and mica schist interspersed with loess deposits. The north bank is steeper and cooler; the south bank at Mautern and Rossatz sees more warmth. The westernmost villages (Spitz and its satellite hamlets in the Spitzer Graben) grow on granite soils with a cool-climate, mineral-driven character. Loiben, Dürnstein and Weissenkirchen in the east benefit from a warmer microclimate and produce rounder, richer Smaragd.

F.X. Pichler, Emmerich Knoll, Prager, Franz Hirtzberger, Alzinger, Nikolaihof and Domäne Wachau have each earned international export distribution for their Smaragd wines. The style is dry, precise, age-worthy and site-specific: the combination of maritime-influenced Atlantic air, warm Pannonian summers and cold nights, ancient gneiss and mica-schist soils, and hand-harvested steep terraces produces a mineral white wine character.

faqs.question: What is the Wachau DAC?
faqs.answer: Wachau DAC (Districtus Austriae Controllatus) is the geographic-origin appellation for wines produced in the Wachau valley, granted in 2020. It covers the 36-kilometre stretch of the Danube between Melk and Krems and allows Grüner Veltliner, Riesling and 15 other traditional varieties. The DAC has three levels: Gebietswein (regional), Ortswein (village) and Riedenwein (single-vineyard).

faqs.question: What are Steinfeder, Federspiel and Smaragd?
faqs.answer: These are the tier marks of the Vinea Wachau growers association, predating the 2020 DAC. Steinfeder is the lightest style (maximum 11.5% alcohol), named after the wild feather grass. Federspiel is mid-weight (11.5 to 12.5%), named after a falconer's lure. Smaragd is the ripest and most concentrated (minimum 12.5%), named after the green emerald lizard found on the warm terrace walls. Non-members of Vinea Wachau use plain Wachau DAC on the label.

faqs.question: Which grape varieties grow in the Wachau?
faqs.answer: Grüner Veltliner and Riesling dominate, sharing approximately 85% of plantings between them. Historic white varieties include Neuburger (unique to the Wachau), Frühroter Veltliner, Roter Veltliner, Gelber Muskateller, Müller-Thurgau, Weissburgunder (Pinot Blanc), Grauburgunder, Chardonnay (Morillon) and Sauvignon Blanc. The Wachau is almost entirely a white-wine region.

faqs.question: When is the best time to visit the Wachau for wine?
faqs.answer: Harvest season (mid-September to late October) is the most atmospheric, with Heurigen wine taverns open and the apricot-and-vine landscape at its most colourful. Spring (April to May) sees the apricot blossom and most estate cellars open. Summer weekends bring river-cruise visitors to Dürnstein and Melk; weekday visits are quieter. The Wachau Marathon weekend in October and the annual Wachau wine spring events attract wine-focused visitors.

faqs.question: How do I visit F.X. Pichler or Emmerich Knoll?
faqs.answer: Both F.X. Pichler (Oberloiben 27, Dürnstein) and Emmerich Knoll (Unterloiben 132, Dürnstein) are by-appointment estates that do not receive general walk-in visitors. Contact them directly via email before your trip. Trade buyers, press and serious wine lovers can usually arrange visits; allocate at least two weeks' lead time during harvest season.
